meta.title: Personal Values Test
meta.description: A Schwartz-style values assessment that reveals what you prioritize when values conflict — like achievement vs. benevolence, or security vs. freedom — across 10 core value dimensions.

categories.label: Self-Direction
categories.desc: Independence and self-direction sit at the center of how you make decisions. You'd rather chart your own course, test your own ideas, and answer to your own judgment than follow someone else's script — even when that means going against the grain.

categories.label: Stimulation
categories.desc: Novelty and challenge keep you engaged with life. Predictable routines wear on you quickly, and you're drawn toward change, variety, and a bit of risk — the unknown feels like an invitation, not a threat.

categories.label: Hedonism
categories.desc: Enjoying life matters to you, and you don't feel the need to justify pleasure with productivity. You give yourself permission to savor comfort, good food, rest, and fun — treating enjoyment as something worth prioritizing, not an afterthought.

categories.label: Achievement
categories.desc: Demonstrating real competence drives you. You set ambitious personal standards, want your effort and skill to show in the results, and feel genuinely motivated by challenges that let you prove what you're capable of.

categories.label: Power
categories.desc: Influence, status, and control over outcomes matter to you. You're comfortable stepping into positions of authority, want your standing to be recognized, and don't shy away from the responsibility that leadership brings.

categories.label: Security
categories.desc: Stability, safety, and harmony anchor how you make decisions. You value predictability in your relationships, finances, and surroundings, and you'll often choose the safer, steadier path over an exciting but uncertain one.

categories.label: Conformity
categories.desc: You place real weight on social expectations and self-restraint. Rather than acting on every impulse, you consider how your behavior affects others and hold yourself to standards of politeness, discipline, and respect for shared norms.

categories.label: Tradition
categories.desc: Customs, faith, and inherited ways of doing things carry deep meaning for you. You find comfort and identity in honoring what's been passed down, and you approach change to long-standing practices with real caution.

categories.label: Benevolence
categories.desc: The wellbeing of the people close to you shapes your choices. You show up reliably for family and friends, value honesty and loyalty in close relationships, and often put their needs on par with — or ahead of — your own.

categories.label: Universalism
categories.desc: Fairness, tolerance, and care extend far beyond your immediate circle for you. You're inclined to stand up for people you've never met, weigh the impact of your choices on the environment, and value equality on principle, not just when it's convenient.

questions.text: You're assigned a project with a rigid template to follow. How likely are you to look for ways to put your own spin on it anyway?
A friend offers unsolicited advice about how to run your life. How likely are you to politely thank them but do it your own way?
You're choosing a career path and your family has a strong opinion. How likely are you to weigh their view but ultimately decide for yourself?
You discover a new hobby that intrigues you, even though no one you know does it. How likely are you to pursue it anyway?
A manager hands you a task with no instructions, just a goal. How likely are you to enjoy figuring out your own approach?
You're offered a well-paying job that requires following strict procedures with no room for creativity. How likely are you to hesitate because of the lack of independence?
You have a strong personal opinion that differs from the group consensus. How likely are you to voice it rather than go along quietly?
You're planning a trip and someone suggests a popular guided tour. How likely are you to prefer exploring on your own itinerary instead?
You're learning a new skill. How likely are you to want to experiment your own way rather than strictly follow a course's steps?
Someone questions why you made an unconventional choice. How likely are you to feel confident explaining that it was your own reasoning, not others'?
You're given two options: a stable role with clear rules, or a freer role with ambiguity. How likely are you to lean toward the freer option?
A creative project you're working on gets "corrected" to match someone else's vision. How likely are you to push back to keep your original idea?
You realize a task could be done faster by copying someone else's exact method. How likely are you to still tweak it to make it your own?
You're deciding how to decorate your own space. How likely are you to choose what genuinely reflects your taste over what's currently trendy?
You could let others make an important personal decision for you just to avoid the effort of deciding. How likely are you to do this?
You're curious about a topic outside your usual field. How likely are you to explore it just because you want to understand it, not because it's required?
Your routine has become extremely predictable and controlled by external schedules. How likely are you to seek out a change that restores your sense of independence?
A group assignment lets everyone pick their own approach. How likely are you to relish having full control over how you tackle your part?
You're asked to follow a strict script during a presentation. How likely are you to feel constrained rather than comfortable?
Someone offers to plan every detail of your weekend. How likely are you to prefer keeping some of it open so you can decide as you go?
You could adopt someone else's opinion as your own without really examining whether you agree. How likely are you to do this?
You're given creative freedom on a project with no oversight. How likely are you to feel energized rather than lost?
A friend tells you that your approach to a problem is unusual. How likely are you to stick with it anyway because it makes sense to you?
You're deciding between a popular, well-trodden path and a less common one that interests you more. How likely are you to choose the one that interests you?
You're asked to memorize and repeat someone else's process exactly. How likely are you to feel restless and want to adapt it?
You could quietly go along with a decision you privately disagree with just to avoid conflict. How likely are you to do this?
You have the chance to design your own study or work schedule. How likely are you to take full advantage of that flexibility?
Someone tells you exactly how they think you should spend your free time. How likely are you to politely disregard it and do what you actually want?
You're exploring a new idea that goes against conventional wisdom. How likely are you to keep investigating it rather than drop it because it's unconventional?
You're asked to choose your own goals for the coming year. How likely are you to set ambitious, personally meaningful ones rather than generic ones?
A rulebook at work leaves little room for individual judgment. How likely are you to find it frustrating rather than reassuring?
You're given the option to learn a skill through free experimentation or a strict step-by-step course. How likely are you to choose free experimentation?
You could avoid trying anything new because it's easier to just do what everyone else does. How likely are you to do this?
You have a strong instinct about how to solve a problem that differs from the standard method. How likely are you to trust your own instinct?
Someone tries to talk you out of an unconventional life choice you feel good about. How likely are you to stay the course anyway?
You're free to choose your own reading material for a class project. How likely are you to pick something that genuinely interests you over an easy option?
A situation calls for you to think outside pre-approved options. How likely are you to enjoy generating your own alternative rather than picking from the list?
You've been offered a mentor who wants to shape your decisions closely. How likely are you to want independence to make your own mistakes and learn?
You could let a strong personality in your life quietly make most of your decisions for you. How likely are you to do this?
You're deciding how to spend an unplanned free day. How likely are you to follow your own curiosity rather than a preset plan?
Your workplace introduces more autonomy in how tasks are completed. How likely are you to feel more motivated because of the added freedom?
You come up with an original idea that no one asked for. How likely are you to bring it forward anyway because you believe in it?
A rigid tradition in your family conflicts with how you'd prefer to do something. How likely are you to gently do it your own way?
You're given a choice between following a proven recipe exactly or adapting it with your own twist. How likely are you to adapt it?
Someone offers to make an important life decision on your behalf to save you the trouble. How likely are you to decline and decide for yourself?
You notice a more efficient way to do something that breaks from the "approved" method. How likely are you to try it your way regardless?
You could stop pursuing your own interests simply because they don't match what's popular right now. How likely are you to do this?
You're choosing between two similar jobs — one with more freedom, one with more security but less autonomy. How likely are you to lean toward freedom?
A creative outlet lets you set your own rules entirely. How likely are you to feel most like yourself while doing it?
You realize your goals have quietly become someone else's goals for you. How likely are you to take steps to reclaim your own direction?
You're offered a routine, safe assignment or a challenging one with an uncertain outcome. How likely are you to pick the challenging one for the thrill of it?
A weekend trip is proposed with a totally new, unfamiliar destination. How likely are you to jump at the chance over a familiar favorite?
Your daily routine has become extremely predictable. How likely are you to actively seek out something new to break the monotony?
You're offered the chance to try an extreme sport you've never attempted. How likely are you to say yes despite the risk?
A new, unproven approach to a project promises to be more exciting, even if riskier. How likely are you to choose it over the tried-and-true method?
You're bored on a free evening. How likely are you to seek out a spontaneous, unplanned adventure rather than stay in with something familiar?
Someone invites you to a completely unfamiliar cultural event. How likely are you to go purely out of curiosity and excitement?
You could turn down an exciting opportunity simply because it disrupts your comfortable routine. How likely are you to do this?
A job offer promises constant variety and unpredictable challenges. How likely are you to find that appealing rather than stressful?
You've done the same hobby for years. How likely are you to want to try something completely different just for the novelty?
You're given a choice of travel itineraries: one relaxing and predictable, one packed with unknowns. How likely are you to pick the unpredictable one?
A friend dares you to try something a little risky but thrilling. How likely are you to take them up on it?
You could stick to the same restaurant and order every time rather than try something new. How likely are you to do this?
You're offered a chance to learn an intense, physically demanding new skill. How likely are you to embrace the challenge?
Your life has felt stagnant lately. How likely are you to make a bold change just to shake things up?
A new project at work has an unclear, unpredictable path. How likely are you to feel excited rather than anxious about the uncertainty?
You're deciding between a calm staycation and a spontaneous road trip with no fixed plan. How likely are you to choose the road trip?
Someone offers you a thrilling but slightly nerve-wracking experience, like public speaking on a topic you've never covered. How likely are you to accept?
You could avoid trying a new cuisine because it's simpler to order what you already know you like. How likely are you to do this?
You notice your schedule has become too comfortable and repetitive. How likely are you to intentionally add something unpredictable to it?
A once-in-a-lifetime, slightly risky opportunity arises, like an impromptu overseas move. How likely are you to seriously consider it?
You're choosing an activity for a get-together. How likely are you to suggest something none of you have tried before?
A friend describes a hobby as intense and unpredictable. How likely are you to want to try it because of that description, not despite it?
You could decline an adventurous invitation just because it feels safer to stay home. How likely are you to do this?
You're offered creative work that guarantees excitement but no job security. How likely are you to find that trade-off worth it?
Your usual social circle rarely tries anything new. How likely are you to be the one pushing for a novel experience?
You have the chance to learn something completely outside your expertise, with no guarantee you'll be good at it. How likely are you to dive in anyway?
A spontaneous last-minute plan disrupts your evening. How likely are you to embrace it rather than feel annoyed?
You could pass on an unfamiliar experience because sticking to what you know feels easier. How likely are you to do this?
You're deciding between two vacation options: one with a packed, unpredictable itinerary, one that's relaxed and fully planned. How likely are you to choose the unpredictable one?
Someone suggests trying an activity that feels a little intimidating, like an obstacle course or a new sport. How likely are you to go for it?
Your job becomes unexpectedly repetitive. How likely are you to actively look for ways to add challenge and variety back in?
You're given the option to take on a high-stakes, high-thrill task versus a safe, comfortable one. How likely are you to choose the thrilling one?
You could keep choosing the exact same weekend routine every week because it's comfortable. How likely are you to do this?
A friend proposes an impulsive weekend getaway with no real plan. How likely are you to say yes on the spot?
You notice you feel most alive when trying something unfamiliar. How likely are you to chase that feeling more often?
A challenge is presented with real risk of failure but a thrilling potential payoff. How likely are you to take it on?
You're offered two projects: one predictable and low-risk, one bold and uncertain. How likely are you to choose the bold one?
You could avoid an unfamiliar social event because staying in your comfort zone feels safer. How likely are you to do this?
Your usual vacation spot is available again this year. How likely are you to pick somewhere completely new instead, just for the novelty?
You're invited to try something that could either be amazing or a total flop. How likely are you to take the gamble?
Life has felt too calm for your taste lately. How likely are you to actively seek out an adrenaline-inducing experience?
A friend describes their recent spontaneous decision that upended their routine. How likely are you to feel inspired to do something similarly bold?
You could turn down a novel experience just because you don't know exactly what to expect. How likely are you to do this?
You're deciding between learning a stable, useful skill or an unusual, thrilling one with less practical value. How likely are you to choose the thrilling one?
Someone proposes exploring an unfamiliar part of town late at night, just for the experience. How likely are you to go along?
Your team suggests trying a completely new, untested strategy on a project. How likely are you to be enthusiastic about it?
You've been offered the chance to swap your predictable job for a role with constant new challenges. How likely are you to take it?
You could avoid changing your routine even when it's grown stale, because change feels like too much effort. How likely are you to do this?
You're bored with your usual weekend pattern. How likely are you to deliberately plan something completely out of character?
You've had a stressful week. How likely are you to treat yourself to something purely enjoyable, even if it's a little indulgent?
You're deciding between a productive chore and a relaxing, pleasurable activity. How likely are you to choose the pleasurable one when you have the choice?
A dessert looks incredibly tempting after a filling meal. How likely are you to enjoy it anyway rather than resist for practical reasons?
You have a free evening. How likely are you to prioritize something purely fun over something "useful"?
Someone suggests a spa day just for relaxation and pleasure, no other purpose. How likely are you to happily go along?
You're on vacation with no obligations. How likely are you to spend the day doing exactly what feels good, without guilt?
You could deny yourself a small, harmless pleasure just because you feel you should always be productive. How likely are you to do this?
You're offered a comfortable, luxurious option and a plain, practical one at the same price. How likely are you to choose comfort and enjoyment?
A friend invites you to a leisurely, indulgent brunch instead of a quick, efficient one. How likely are you to choose the leisurely option?
You have some unexpected free time. How likely are you to use it purely for enjoyment rather than catching up on tasks?
You could feel guilty about enjoying a treat and cut your enjoyment of it short. How likely are you to do this?
You're choosing a hotel room for a trip. How likely are you to splurge on comfort and pleasure over saving money?
Someone offers you a chance to relax fully for an afternoon with no responsibilities. How likely are you to take it without hesitation?
You're tired after a long day. How likely are you to indulge in something soothing, like a favorite show or meal, without feeling you need to "earn" it?
A pleasurable activity conflicts slightly with your to-do list. How likely are you to let yourself enjoy it anyway?
You could push through exhaustion refusing any small comfort because you think you don't deserve a break yet. How likely are you to do this?
You're deciding what to eat. How likely are you to choose what genuinely sounds delicious over what's simply the healthiest, "correct" option?
Someone gifts you a spontaneous day of pure leisure. How likely are you to enjoy it fully rather than feel restless about being unproductive?
You have the option to unwind with something purely pleasurable after finishing a task. How likely are you to take that time for yourself?
You could skip a small personal indulgence, like a favorite snack or show, because you feel it's frivolous. How likely are you to do this?
You're planning your ideal weekend. How likely are you to build it entirely around things that simply feel good?
A friend suggests treating yourselves to something a little extravagant just because. How likely are you to enjoy the idea rather than talk yourselves out of it?
You're offered a comfortable seat upgrade for a modest fee. How likely are you to take it for the extra comfort?
You could avoid a relaxing massage or treat because you feel your time is better spent being productive. How likely are you to do this?
You finish a big project. How likely are you to celebrate with something purely enjoyable rather than move straight to the next task?
Someone offers you the last piece of a dessert you love. How likely are you to happily accept and savor it?
You have a choice between a quick, efficient errand route and a scenic, pleasant one that takes longer. How likely are you to take the scenic route?
You could rush through a pleasant experience, like a nice meal, just to get back to being productive. How likely are you to do this?
You're deciding how to spend a bonus. How likely are you to spend some of it on something purely for enjoyment?
A lazy Sunday morning with nothing planned appeals to you. How likely are you to fully embrace it rather than fill it with chores?
You're offered the chance to sleep in versus getting up early to be productive on a day off. How likely are you to sleep in and enjoy it?
You could feel that resting and having fun is a waste of time that should be spent working instead. How likely are you to feel this way?
Someone suggests a fun, frivolous activity with no productive purpose. How likely are you to enthusiastically join in?
You're choosing music for a relaxing evening. How likely are you to pick whatever brings you the most enjoyment, regardless of anyone else's taste?
A comfortable but slightly more expensive option is available for a purchase. How likely are you to choose comfort and pleasure over pure practicality?
You could deny yourself downtime after a hard week because you feel you haven't "earned" it yet. How likely are you to do this?
You have the chance to indulge in a favorite hobby purely for fun, with no goal attached. How likely are you to do it just because it feels good?
Your schedule allows for a pleasurable detour on the way home. How likely are you to take it just to enjoy the moment?
You could turn down a pleasant, relaxing offer because you feel you should be doing something more "worthwhile." How likely are you to do this?
You're deciding between a practical gift for yourself and a purely fun, indulgent one. How likely are you to choose the fun one?
A friend treats you to something enjoyable with no strings attached. How likely are you to accept and enjoy it fully?
You have a free hour. How likely are you to spend it doing something simply because it feels good, not because it's productive?
You could feel that taking time to enjoy yourself is selfish or wasteful. How likely are you to feel this way?
You're offered dessert before dinner, breaking the usual order. How likely are you to go along with it just for the fun of it?
A pleasurable, low-effort evening is available instead of a demanding but "important" one. How likely are you to choose the pleasurable evening when you have the option?
You've finished your responsibilities for the day. How likely are you to spend the rest of it purely enjoying yourself without guilt?
You could feel you need to justify every pleasurable choice with a productive reason. How likely are you to feel this way?
You're deciding how to unwind after a hard week. How likely are you to choose whatever brings you the most genuine enjoyment?
Someone offers to handle your errands so you can simply relax. How likely are you to accept and enjoy the free time?
You have a chance to extend a fun outing a little longer, even though it's not strictly necessary. How likely are you to stay and enjoy it?
You're given a challenging goal at work with a clear measure of success. How likely are you to push hard to excel at it?
Two people are being considered for a big opportunity. How likely are you to actively make the case for why you deserve it based on your abilities?
You've mastered a skill and want to prove it. How likely are you to seek out a way to demonstrate that mastery to others?
Your performance is compared against a personal best. How likely are you to be motivated to beat that number?
You're offered an easier version of a task or a harder one that would be more impressive if completed. How likely are you to choose the harder one?
Someone recognizes your accomplishment publicly. How likely are you to feel genuinely motivated to achieve even more?
You could settle for mediocre results on something you know you could excel at, just to avoid the extra effort. How likely are you to do this?
You're setting goals for the year. How likely are you to set ambitious targets that will stretch your abilities?
A competition is announced in an area where you're skilled. How likely are you to enter, wanting to prove your competence?
You finish a project successfully. How likely are you to want it acknowledged rather than let it go unnoticed?
You could avoid a challenging task altogether because failing to succeed at it would bother you less than not trying. How likely are you to do this?
You're offered feedback that your work is merely "good enough." How likely are you to push yourself to make it genuinely excellent?
A colleague is recognized for achievements similar to yours. How likely are you to feel driven to reach that same level, or exceed it?
You have the chance to take credit for a difficult accomplishment you earned. How likely are you to make sure people know it was your doing?
You could downplay a real accomplishment just to avoid drawing attention to your success. How likely are you to do this?
You're deciding between an easy win and a difficult challenge that would showcase real skill. How likely are you to go for the difficult challenge?
Your work is being evaluated against a high standard. How likely are you to rise to meet or exceed that standard?
You're offered a role explicitly because of a track record of achievement. How likely are you to feel proud and motivated to keep that record going?
A skill you have could be used to win a competitive advantage. How likely are you to use it deliberately to come out on top?
You could quietly accept losing to someone less capable, without trying to demonstrate your own competence. How likely are you to do this?
You're given a stretch assignment that could make or break your reputation. How likely are you to take it on as a chance to prove yourself?
Someone doubts your ability to accomplish a difficult goal. How likely are you to work extra hard specifically to prove them wrong?
You're comparing your progress to peers in your field. How likely are you to use that comparison as motivation to work harder?
You could avoid setting personal goals altogether because not achieving them would feel worse than never trying. How likely are you to do this?
You finish first in a competitive task. How likely are you to feel a strong sense of pride in the accomplishment?
You're offered recognition for a job well done. How likely are you to feel that recognition matters to you, not just the outcome itself?
A difficult certification or qualification is available in your field. How likely are you to pursue it as proof of your capability?
You could let an opportunity to showcase a hard-earned skill pass by without effort. How likely are you to do this?
You're deciding how much effort to put into a task no one is likely to notice. How likely are you to still aim for excellence?
Someone praises your talent but says you could go further. How likely are you to take that as motivation to push yourself harder?
A high-visibility project needs a lead. How likely are you to volunteer, seeing it as a chance to prove your capability?
You could feel indifferent about whether your hard work at something ever gets recognized. How likely are you to feel this way?
You're offered a shortcut that would get results faster but wouldn't showcase your actual skill. How likely are you to skip the shortcut for the sake of a more impressive accomplishment?
You set a personal record in something you care about. How likely are you to feel genuinely satisfied and want to beat it again?
You could stop striving for a goal the moment it stops being easy. How likely are you to do this?
You're given the chance to lead a difficult initiative others have avoided. How likely are you to take it as an opportunity to prove your capability?
A mentor challenges you to aim higher than you originally planned. How likely are you to rise to that challenge?
You complete a task perfectly but no one notices. How likely are you to still feel proud of the accomplishment itself?
You could avoid competitive situations altogether because winning or losing doesn't matter much to you. How likely are you to feel this way?
You're deciding whether to pursue an advanced qualification that would set you apart. How likely are you to go for it?
Someone on your team outperforms you on a shared metric. How likely are you to want to work harder to match or beat them?
You have the option to coast through a task or push to do it exceptionally well. How likely are you to push for excellence?
You could feel content never testing the limits of your own ability. How likely are you to feel this way?
You're given the choice between an achievable, modest goal and a stretch goal that would be genuinely impressive. How likely are you to choose the stretch goal?
A difficult project finally comes together successfully. How likely are you to want that success clearly attributed to your effort?
You could avoid difficult tasks specifically because success or failure would reflect directly on your ability. How likely are you to do this?
You're offered public recognition for reaching a milestone. How likely are you to feel that this recognition genuinely motivates you?
Someone underestimates what you're capable of. How likely are you to feel driven to demonstrate your real ability?
You're deciding how to spend your energy this month. How likely are you to channel it toward a goal that will meaningfully advance your skills or standing?
You could feel just as satisfied doing something halfway as doing it excellently, as long as it gets done. How likely are you to feel this way?
You're offered a promotion that comes with authority over a team. How likely are you to want that authority, beyond just the salary increase?
A group needs a leader and no one has stepped up. How likely are you to take charge because you want the influence that comes with it?
You're negotiating a deal. How likely are you to push for terms that give you more control over the outcome, even if it's a harder sell?
Someone challenges your authority in front of others. How likely are you to assert your position firmly rather than let it slide?
You have the chance to influence a decision that affects many people. How likely are you to want to be the one making that call?
You could give up a leadership opportunity because you'd rather not deal with the responsibility of authority. How likely are you to do this?
You're deciding between a high-paying role with real decision-making power and a comfortable one with none. How likely are you to choose the powerful role?
A resource is limited and several people want it. How likely are you to make sure you secure a share of it for yourself?
You're offered a title that carries more prestige, even without much extra pay. How likely are you to want that title?
Someone tries to undermine your position of authority. How likely are you to push back to protect your standing?
You could let someone else take control of a group project even though you'd prefer to be in charge. How likely are you to do this?
You're deciding how to structure a team you lead. How likely are you to make sure key decisions still go through you?
A situation arises where being seen as important matters to the outcome. How likely are you to make sure your role is visible?
You have influence over how resources are distributed. How likely are you to use that influence deliberately to your own advantage?
You could avoid situations where you'd need to assert control over others, even when it's expected of you. How likely are you to do this?
You're offered a chance to be in charge of a high-stakes decision. How likely are you to want that responsibility and the control it brings?
Your social circle looks to someone for leadership. How likely are you to want that person to be you?
You're negotiating your compensation. How likely are you to push hard for a package that reflects real financial power?
You could quietly step back from a leadership role and let a less capable person take over instead. How likely are you to do this?
Someone questions whether you should have the final say in a decision. How likely are you to insist that you do?
You have the chance to build a network of people who owe you favors. How likely are you to actively cultivate that kind of influence?
A group vote could go your way if you push hard enough for it. How likely are you to push for the outcome that benefits your standing?
You could let go of a position of influence rather than fight to keep it, even if you value it. How likely are you to do this?
You're deciding how visible to make your wealth or success. How likely are you to want others to recognize your status?
A rival for a leadership position seems weaker than you. How likely are you to actively compete to make sure you win the position?
You could avoid taking control even in situations where you clearly have the most expertise. How likely are you to do this?
You're offered the chance to set the rules for a group you're part of. How likely are you to want that authority?
Someone in a lower position disagrees with your decision. How likely are you to make clear that the final call is yours?
You have leverage in a negotiation. How likely are you to use it fully to secure the best outcome for yourself?
You could hand over decision-making power to someone else just to avoid the burden of it. How likely are you to do this?
A prestigious role opens up that comes with real authority over budgets and people. How likely are you to pursue it aggressively?
You're deciding how much control to keep over a project versus delegating it fully. How likely are you to keep significant control?
Someone else tries to take credit for a decision that was really yours to make. How likely are you to correct the record?
You could avoid competing for a position of influence, preferring to stay out of the spotlight entirely. How likely are you to do this?
You have the opportunity to expand your influence within an organization. How likely are you to actively pursue that expansion?
A group needs someone to make a final, binding decision. How likely are you to want to be that person?
You're offered a chance to control a valuable resource others depend on. How likely are you to take it, aware of the leverage it gives you?
You could give up a chance at real authority because you don't feel a strong pull toward being in charge. How likely are you to feel this way?
Someone tries to bypass your authority on a decision that's rightfully yours. How likely are you to firmly reassert your position?
You're deciding how much of your success to display publicly. How likely are you to want your status to be visible to others?
A team assignment lets you choose to lead or follow. How likely are you to choose to lead, valuing the control that comes with it?
You could feel uncomfortable holding power over other people's outcomes and try to avoid it. How likely are you to feel this way?
You have the chance to be the deciding vote in an important group decision. How likely are you to relish having that much influence?
Someone with less experience is put above you in a hierarchy. How likely are you to push to correct that arrangement?
You're offered control over a budget that affects several people's work. How likely are you to want that level of authority?
You could pass up an opportunity to gain real influence because you'd rather not be responsible for others. How likely are you to do this?
A negotiation is close, and pushing a little harder could tilt it clearly in your favor. How likely are you to push for that advantage?
You're deciding whether to seek a role with high visibility and authority or one that's quieter and more equal among peers. How likely are you to seek the authoritative role?
You could let your influence in a group fade rather than work to maintain it. How likely are you to do this?
You have a chance to be recognized as the most capable person in the room. How likely are you to want that recognition and the standing it brings?
You're deciding between a stable job with predictable income and an exciting but unstable opportunity. How likely are you to choose the stable job?
Your household routine keeps things running smoothly. How likely are you to protect that routine rather than disrupt it for something new?
You have some savings set aside. How likely are you to keep building that safety net rather than spend it on something exciting?
A relationship in your life feels safe and steady. How likely are you to value that steadiness highly, even over excitement?
You're offered a chance to make a risky investment with high potential reward. How likely are you to pass in favor of a safer option?
Your neighborhood or community feels orderly and safe. How likely are you to want to help preserve that sense of order?
You could take on significant financial risk without a safety net, just because the potential upside excites you. How likely are you to do this?
You're deciding where to live. How likely are you to prioritize a safe, stable environment over a more exciting but less predictable one?
A long-term plan gives you a sense of security about the future. How likely are you to stick to it rather than abandon it for something uncertain?
You notice a potential threat to your family's stability. How likely are you to act quickly to protect it?
You could ignore warning signs about your financial stability because addressing them feels inconvenient. How likely are you to do this?
